\\\"application/xml\\\")\\n1515\\t\\tw.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)\\n1516\\t\\tw.Write([]byte(`<?xml version=\\\"1.0\\\" encoding=\\\"UTF-8\\\"?>`))\\n1517\\t\\tw.Write(output)\\n1518\\t}\\n1519\\t\\n1520\\t// parseCopySource parses the x-amz-copy-source header value.\\n1521\\t// Supports formats: /bucket/key or bucket/key\\n1522\\tfunc parseCopySource(copySource string) (bucket, key string) {\\n1523\\t\\t// Remove leading slash if present\\n1524\\t\\tcopySource = strings.TrimPrefix(copySource, \\\"/\\\")\\n1525\\t\\n1526\\t\\t// URL decode the key portion (keys may contain special characters)\\n1527\\t\\tif idx := strings.Index(copySource, \\\"/\\\"); idx != -1 {\\n1528\\t\\t\\tbucket = copySource[:idx]\\n1529\\t\\t\\tkey = copySource[idx+1:]\\n1530\\t\\t\\t// URL decode the key\\n1531\\t\\t\\tif decoded, err := url.QueryUnescape(key); err == nil {\\n1532\\t\\t\\t\\tkey = decoded\\n1533\\t\\t\\t}\\n1534\\t\\t}\\n1535\\t\\n1536\\t\\treturn bucket, key\\n1537\\t}\\n1538\\t\\n1539\\t// enrichPlaintextSizes replaces encrypted on-disk sizes with plaintext sizes for\\n1540\\t// ARMOR-encrypted objects. B2's ListObjectsV2 response includes the encrypted\\n1541\\t// object size; we resolve the plaintext size using the metadata cache (if warm)\\n1542\\t// or a parallel HeadObject call per object (on cache miss). Each errgroup goroutine\\n1543\\t// writes to a distinct slice index so no mutex is needed.\\n1544\\tfunc (h *Handlers) enrichPlaintextSizes(ctx context.Context, bucket string, result *backend.ListResult) {\\n1545\\t\\teg, egCtx := errgroup.WithContext(ctx)\\n1546\\t\\tfor i := range result.Objects {\\n1547\\t\\t\\ti := i\\n1548\\t\\t\\tkey := result.Objects[i].Key\\n1549\\t\\t\\tif am, ok := h.cache.Get(bucket, key); ok {\\n1550\\t\\t\\t\\tif am != nil && am.PlaintextSize > 0 {\\n1551\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tresult.Objects[i].Size = am.PlaintextSize\\n1552\\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n1553\\t\\t\\t\\tcontinue\\n1554\\t\\t\\t}\\n1555\\t\\t\\teg.Go(func() error {\\n1556\\t\\t\\t\\tinfo, err := h.backend.Head(egCtx, bucket, key)\\n1557\\t\\t\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n1558\\t\\t\\t\\t\\treturn nil // non-fatal: keep encrypted size rather than failing the listing\\n1559\\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n1560\\t\\t\\t\\tif info.Size > 0 {\\n1561\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tresult.Objects[i].Size = info.Size\\n1562\\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n1563\\t\\t\\t\\treturn nil\\n1564\\t\\t\\t})\\n1565\\t\\t}\\n1566\\t\\teg.Wait() //nolint:errcheck\\n1567\\t}\\n1568\\t\\n1569\\t// ListObjectsV2 handles S3 ListObjectsV2.\\n1570\\tfunc (h *Handlers) ListObjectsV2(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request, bucket string) {\\n1571\\t\\tctx := r.Context()\\n1572\\t\\n1573\\t\\tprefix := r.URL.Query().Get(\\\"prefix\\\")\\n1574\\t\\tdelimiter := r.URL.Query().Get(\\\"delimiter\\\")\\n1575\\t\\tcontToken := r.URL.Query().Get(\\\"continuation-token\\\")\\n1576\\t\\tmaxKeys := 1000\\n1577\\t\\tif mk := r.URL.Query().Get(\\\"max-keys\\\"); mk != \\\"\\\" {\\n1578\\t\\t\\tif v, err := strconv.Atoi(mk); err == nil && v > 0 {\\n1579\\t\\t\\t\\tmaxKeys = v\\n1580\\t\\t\\t}\\n1581\\t\\t}\\n1582\\t\\n1583\\t\\t// Apply the configured prefix to the prefix parameter for backend operations.\\n1584\\t\\t// When ARMOR_PREFIX is set, the backend stores all keys with the prefix prepended,\\n1585\\t\\t// but clients don't know about it. We need to prepend the prefix to the client's\\n1586\\t\\t// requested prefix so the backend finds the right objects.\\n1587\\t\\tbackendPrefix := h.applyPrefix(prefix)\\n1588\\t\\n1589\\t\\tvar result *backend.ListResult\\n1590\\t\\tif h.listCache != nil {\\n1591\\t\\t\\tif cached, ok := h.listCache.Get(bucket, backendPrefix, delimiter, maxKeys, contToken); ok {\\n1592\\t\\t\\t\\tresult = cached\\n1593\\t\\t\\t}\\n1594\\t\\t}\\n1595\\t\\tif result == nil {\\n1596\\t\\t\\tvar err error\\n1597\\t\\t\\tresult, err = h.backend.List(ctx, bucket, backendPrefix, delimiter, contToken, maxKeys)\\n1598\\t\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n1599\\t\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to list: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n1600\\t\\t\\t\\treturn\\n1601\\t\\t\\t}\\n1602\\t\\t\\t// Resolve plaintext sizes for ARMOR-encrypted objects before caching.\\n1603\\t\\t\\t// B2 ListObjectsV2 returns encrypted (on-disk) sizes; clients like DuckDB\\n1604\\t\\t\\t// use these sizes to compute byte-range offsets, so they must reflect the\\n1605\\t\\t\\t// plaintext length.\\n1606\\t\\t\\th.enrichPlaintextSizes(ctx, bucket, result)\\n1607\\t\\t\\tif h.listCache != nil {\\n1608\\t\\t\\t\\th.listCache.Set(bucket, prefix, delimiter, maxKeys, contToken, result)\\n1609\\t\\t\\t}\\n1610\\t\\t}\\n1611\\t\\n1612\\t\\t// Build XML response\\n1613\\t\\ttype Contents struct {\\n1614\\t\\t\\tKey          string `xml:\\\"Key\\\"`\\n1615\\t\\t\\tLastModified string `xml:\\\"LastModified\\\"`\\n1616\\t\\t\\tETag         string `xml:\\\"ETag\\\"`\\n1617\\t\\t\\tSize         int64  `xml:\\\"Size\\\"`\\n1618\\t\\t\\tStorageClass string `xml:\\\"StorageClass\\\"`\\n1619\\t\\t}\\n1620\\t\\n1621\\t\\t// S3 spec: each CommonPrefixes entry is a separate wrapper element with one\\n1622\\t\\t// Prefix child. Using []string with \\\"Outer>Inner\\\" produces an empty\\n1623\\t\\t// <CommonPrefixes/> when the slice is nil, which the AWS SDK v1 parses as a\\n1624\\t\\t// slice with one nil element — causing nil-pointer crashes in clients like\\n1625\\t\\t// litestream. Using a dedicated struct + omitempty avoids this.\\n1626\\t\\ttype CommonPrefix struct {\\n1627\\t\\t\\tPrefix string `xml:\\\"Prefix\\\"`\\n1628\\t\\t}\\n1629\\t\\n1630\\t\\ttype ListBucketResult  \\\"http://s3.amazonaws.com/doc/2006-03-01/\\\",\\n1645\\t\\t\\tName:                  bucket,\\n1646\\t\\t\\tPrefix:                prefix,\\n1647\\t\\t\\tDelimiter: {\\n1922\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", \\\"Failed to marshal response\\\", 500)\\n1923\\t\\t\\treturn\\n1924\\t\\t}\\n1925\\t\\n1926\\t\\tw.Header().Set(\\\"Content-Type\\\", encoding=\\\"UTF-8\\\"?>`))\\n1929\\t\\tw.Write(output)\\n1930\\t}\\n1931\\t\\n1932\\t// CreateMultipartUpload handles S3 CreateMultipartUpload with ARMOR encryption.\\n1933\\t// It generates a DEK and IV, wraps the DEK, and stores the state in B2.\\n1934\\tfunc (h *Handlers) CreateMultipartUpload(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request, bucket, key string) {\\n1935\\t\\tctx := r.Context()\\n1936\\t\\n1937\\t\\t// Get the appropriate MEK for this object key\\n1938\\t\\tmek, keyID, err := h.keyManager.GetMEK(key)\\n1939\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n1940\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"application/octet-stream\\\"\\n1968\\t\\t}\\n1969\\t\\n1970\\t\\t// Initiate multipart upload with B2 (no ARMOR metadata yet - that comes on completion)\\n1971\\t\\tuploadID, err := h.backend.CreateMultipartUpload(ctx, bucket, key, nil)\\n1972\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n1973\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to create multipart upload: %v\\\", 0,\\n1989\\t\\t\\tPartHMACs:      make(map[int]string),\\n1990\\t\\t\\tPartSizes:      make(map[int]int64),\\n1991\\t\\t}\\n1992\\t\\n1993\\t\\tmanager := backend.NewMultipartStateManager(h.backend, key, uploadID)\\n1997\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to save multipart state: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n1998\\t\\t\\treturn\\n1999\\t\\t}\\n2000\\t\\n2001\\t\\t// Build XML response\\n2002\\t\\ttype InitiateMultipartUploadResult struct {\\n2003\\t\\t\\tXMLName  xml.Name `xml:\\\"InitiateMultipartUploadResult\\\"`\\n2004\\t\\t\\tXmlns    string encoding=\\\"UTF-8\\\"?>`))\\n2026\\t\\tw.Write(output)\\n2027\\t}\\n2028\\t\\n2029\\t// UploadPart handles S3 UploadPart with encryption.\\n2030\\t// Each part is encrypted with a CTR counter offset based on cumulative encrypted bytes.\\n2031\\tfunc (h *Handlers) UploadPart(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request, bucket, key, uploadID string) {\\n2032\\t\\tctx := r.Context()\\n2033\\t\\n2034\\t\\t// Parse part number\\n2035\\t\\tpartNumberStr := r.URL.Query().Get(\\\"partNumber\\\")\\n2036\\t\\tif partNumberStr == \\\"\\\" {\\n2037\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InvalidRequest\\\", \\\"Missing partNumber\\\", 400)\\n2038\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2039\\t\\t}\\n2040\\t\\tpartNumber, err := strconv.ParseInt(partNumberStr, 10, 32)\\n2041\\t\\tif err != nil || partNumber < 1 || partNumber > 10000 {\\n2042\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InvalidRequest\\\", \\\"Invalid partNumber\\\", 400)\\n2043\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2044\\t\\t}\\n2045\\t\\n2046\\t\\t// Load multipart state\\n2047\\t\\tmanager := backend.NewMultipartStateManager(h.backend, bucket)\\n2048\\t\\tstate, err := manager.LoadState(ctx, uploadID)\\n2049\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n2050\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"NoSuchUpload\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Multipart upload not found: %v\\\", err), 404)\\n2051\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2052\\t\\t}\\n2053\\t\\n2054\\t\\t// Verify bucket and key 404)\\n2057\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2058\\t\\t}\\n2059\\t\\n2060\\t\\t// Read plaintext part\\n2061\\t\\tplaintext, err := io.ReadAll(r.Body)\\n2062\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n2063\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to read body: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n2064\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2065\\t\\t}\\n2066\\t\\n2067\\t\\tplaintextSize := int64(len(plaintext))\\n2068\\t\\n2069\\t\\t// VALIDATION: Reject unsupported multipart patterns that would cause silent corruption\\n2070\\t\\t// See docs/upload-retrieval-test-matrix.md U6/U7/U8 and bead bf-59unr3\\n2071\\t\\n2072\\t\\t// U7: Reject part retries (same partNumber uploaded more than once)\\n2073\\t\\tif _, exists := state.PartSizes[int(partNumber)]; exists {\\n2074\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InvalidPart\\\",\\n2075\\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\\"Part %d has already been uploaded. ARMOR does not support part retries due to CTR counter derivation from cumulative bytes. Retry the entire multipart upload from the beginning.\\\", partNumber), 400)\\n2076\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2077\\t\\t}\\n2078\\t\\n2079\\t\\t// U6: Reject out-of-order parts (parts must be uploaded sequentially)\\n2080\\t\\t// The expected part number is derived from the count of already-uploaded parts\\n2081\\t\\texpectedPartNumber := len(state.PartSizes) + 1\\n2082\\t\\tif int(partNumber) != expectedPartNumber {\\n2083\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InvalidPartOrder\\\",\\n2084\\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\\"Parts must be uploaded in sequential order. Expected part %d, got part %d. ARMOR does not support out-of-order or concurrent part uploads due to CTR counter derivation from cumulative bytes.\\\", expectedPartNumber, partNumber), 400)\\n2085\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2086\\t\\t}\\n2087\\t\\n2088\\t\\t// U8: Reject non-block-aligned intermediate parts\\n2089\\t\\t// All parts except the last must be multiples of BlockSize (65536 bytes for AES)\\n2090\\t\\t// We don't know if this is the last part at upload time, so we enforce alignment for all parts.\\n2091\\t\\t// Clients can work around this by using a part size that's a multiple of BlockSize.\\n2092\\t\\tif plaintextSize > 0 && plaintextSize%int64(state.BlockSize) != 0 {\\n2093\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InvalidPartSize\\\",\\n2094\\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\\"Part size %d is not a multiple of the block size (%d bytes). ARMOR's CTR counter derivation requires all parts to be block-aligned. Use a part size that's a multiple of %d (e.g., 5,242,880 for 5MiB, 16,777,216 for 16MiB).\\\", plaintextSize, state.BlockSize, state.BlockSize), 400)\\n2095\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2096\\t\\t}\\n2097\\t\\n2098\\t\\t// Get the MEK for this upload using the key ID from state\\n2099\\t\\tmek, err := h.keyManager.GetMEKByID(state.KeyID)\\n2100\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n2101\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to get decryption key: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n2102\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2103\\t\\t}\\n2104\\t\\n2105\\t\\t// Unwrap DEK\\n2106\\t\\tdek, err := crypto.UnwrapDEK(mek, state.WrappedDEK)\\n2107\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n2108\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to unwrap DEK: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n2109\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2110\\t\\t}\\n2111\\t\\n2112\\t\\t// Calculate starting block index for CTR counter\\n2113\\t\\t// Each block is blockSize bytes, so counter = encryptedBytes / blockSize\\n2114\\t\\tstartBlockIndex := uint32(state.EncryptedBytes / int64(state.BlockSize))\\n2115\\t\\n2116\\t\\t// Create encryptor\\n2117\\t\\tencryptor, err := crypto.NewEncryptor(dek, state.IV, state.BlockSize)\\n2118\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n2119\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to create encryptor: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n2120\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2121\\t\\t}\\n2122\\t\\n2123\\t\\t// Encrypt the part with the correct starting counter\\n2124\\t\\t// For multipart uploads, each part continues the CTR stream from where\\n2125\\t\\t// the previous part left off to maintain cryptographic continuity\\n2126\\t\\tencrypted, blockHMACsRaw, err := encryptor.EncryptWithStartingCounter(plaintext, startBlockIndex)\\n2127\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n2128\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to encrypt: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n2129\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2130\\t\\t}\\n2131\\t\\n2132\\t\\t// Upload encrypted part to B2\\n2133\\t\\tetag, err := h.backend.UploadPart(ctx, bucket, key, uploadID, int32(partNumber), bytes.NewReader(encrypted), int64(len(encrypted)))\\n2134\\t\\tif err != nil {\\n2135\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to upload part: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n2136\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2137\\t\\t}\\n2138\\t\\n2139\\t\\t// Split concatenated HMACs into individual HMACs for storage\\n2140\\t\\t// The EncryptWithStartingCounter method returns HMACs as a flat byte slice\\n2141\\t\\tblockCount := len(blockHMACsRaw) / crypto.HMACSize\\n2142\\t\\tblockHMACs := make([][]byte, blockCount)\\n2143\\t\\tfor i := 0; i < blockCount; i++ {\\n2144\\t\\t\\tblockHMACs[i] = blockHMACsRaw[i*crypto.HMACSize : (i+1)*crypto.HMACSize]\\n2145\\t\\t}\\n2146\\t\\n2147\\t\\t// Update state with cumulative bytes and part HMACs\\n2148\\t\\tstate.EncryptedBytes += int64(len(encrypted))\\n2149\\t\\tstate.PartHMACs[int(partNumber)] = backend.EncodeHMACToBase64(blockHMACs)\\n2150\\t\\tstate.PartSizes[int(partNumber)] = plaintextSize\\n2151\\t\\n2152\\t\\tif err := manager.SaveState(ctx, state); err != nil {\\n2153\\t\\t\\th.writeError(w, \\\"InternalError\\\", fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Failed to update multipart state: %v\\\", err), 500)\\n2154\\t\\t\\treturn\\n2155\\t\\t}\\n2156\\t\\n2157\\t\\tw.Header().Set(\\\"ETag\\\", etag)\\n2158\\t\\tw.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)\\n2159\\t}\\n2160\\t\\n2161\\t// validate\\n2\\t\\n3\\t/*\\n4\\tPackage validate provides a standardized, extensible validation error format for HTTP API testing.\\n5\\t\\n6\\tThis package defines the ValidationError data structure, which offers a comprehensive, machine-readable\\n7\\tformat for representing validation failures across different types of API validations (HTTP status codes,\\n8\\terror message patterns, content types, response structures, CORS headers, and more).\\n9\\t\\n10\\t# Validation Error Format\\n11\\t\\n12\\tThe ValidationError struct is the core of this package's error reporting system. It follows these design\\n13\\tprinciples:\\n14\\t\\n15\\t  - Serializable: Full JSON marshaling/unmarshaling support via struct tags\\n16\\t  - Machine-readable: Clear field names suitable for automated processing and log aggregation\\n17\\t  - Human-readable: Descriptive messages with helpful suggestions for debugging\\n18\\t  - Extensible: Optional fields provide additional context without breaking existing code\\n19\\t\\n20\\t# Required vs Optional Fields\\n21\\t\\n22\\tRequired fields that must be set for every ValidationError:\\n23\\t  - ErrorType: The validation category (e.g., \\\"status_code\\\", \\\"error_message\\\", \\\"content_type\\\")\\n24\\t  - Message: A concise, human-readable description of the validation failure\\n25\\t\\n26\\tOptional fields that provide additional context:\\n27\\t  - Expected/Actual: The values that were expected and actually received\\n28\\t  - Context: Where/when the validation occurred (e.g., endpoint, operation type)\\n29\\t  - FieldName: Specific field where the error was found (e.g., in response body JSON)\\n30\\t  - Location: Position information (e.g., \\\"line 5\\\", \\\"field 'user.email'\\\")\\n31\\t  - RelatedFields: Other fields related to this error for additional context\\n32\\t  - PatternDetails: Information about regex pattern matching failures\\n33\\t  - RangeInfo: Range boundaries for range-based validations (e.g., \\\"4xx\\\", \\\"5xx\\\")\\n34\\t  - ValidationDetails: Additional validation-specific information as a list\\n35\\t  - ResponseSnippet: Truncated excerpt from the response for debugging\\n36\\t  - Suggestions: Actionable recommendations for resolving the error\\n37\\t\\n38\\t# Creating Validation Errors\\n39\\t\\n40\\tThis package provides several ways to create ValidationError instances:\\n41\\t\\n42\\t  1. Direct struct initialization: Create ValidationError directly for full control\\n43\\t  2. ValidationFormatter builder: Use the builder pattern for ergonomic, fluent construction\\n44\\t  3. Convenience functions: Use pre-built functions like FormatStatusCodeError for common cases\\n45\\t  4. Custom validation: Use FormatCustomValidationError with FormatOption for advanced scenarios\\n46\\t\\n47\\tExample usage:\\n48\\t\\n49\\t\\t// Using the ValidationFormatter builder\\n50\\t\\terr := NewValidationFormatter(\\\"status_code\\\").\\n51\\t\\t\\tWithExpected(200).\\n52\\t\\t\\tWithActual(404).\\n53\\t\\t\\tWithContext(\\\"GET /api/users/123\\\").\\n54\\t\\t\\tFormat()\\n55\\t\\t// Output:\\n56\\t\\t// status_code validation failed\\n57\\t\\t//   Expected: 200 (OK)\\n58\\t\\t//   Actual:   404 (Not Found)\\n59\\t\\t//   Context:  GET /api/users/123\\n60\\t\\t//   Suggestions:\\n61\\t\\t//     - Verify the endpoint URL is correct\\n62\\t\\t//     - Check if the resource ID or identifier exists\\n63\\t\\n64\\t\\t// Using a convenience function\\n65\\t\\terr := FormatStatusCodeError(200, 404, \\\"GET /api/users/123\\\")\\n66\\t\\n67\\t# Error Interface\\n68\\t\\n69\\tValidationError implements the error interface, producing detailed, multi-line error messages\\n70\\tthat include all relevant validation information. The Error() method formats the output\\n71\\twith expected/actual values, context, field information, and suggestions for resolution.\\n72\\t\\n73\\t# Serialization\\n74\\t\\n75\\tValidationError includes JSON struct tags for all fields, enabling easy serialization:\\n76\\t\\n77\\t\\terr := ValidationError{ErrorType: \\\"status_code\\\", Message: \\\"test\\\"}\\n78\\t\\tjsonBytes, _ := json.Marshal(err)\\n79\\t\\t// Output: {\\\"error_type\\\":\\\"status_code\\\",\\\"message\\\":\\\"test\\\"}\\n80\\t\\n81\\tThe ToMap() method provides flexible map[string]interface{} conversion for dynamic\\n82\\tmanipulation before serialization.\\n83\\t\\n84\\t# Validation\\n85\\t\\n86\\tThe Validate() method checks that a ValidationError is properly structured with\\n87\\trequired fields populated. Use this to catch programming errors early:\\n88\\t\\n89\\t\\terr := ValidationError{ErrorType: \\\"status_code\\\", Message: \\\"test\\\"}\\n90\\t\\tif err := err.Validate(); err != nil {\\n91\\t\\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"Invalid error structure: %v\\\", err)\\n92\\t\\t}\\n93\\t*/\\n94\\t\\n95\\timport (\\n96\\t\\t\\\"fmt\\\"\\n97\\t\\t\\\"strings\\\"\\n98\\t)\\n99\\t\\n100\\t// =============================================================================\\n101\\t// HELPER TYPES\\n102\\t// =============================================================================\\n103\\t\\n104\\t// ValidationErrorData is a simplified, serialization-friendly version of ValidationError.\\n105\\t// It provides a consistent format for external communication and API responses.\\n106\\t// This type is designed to be easily marshaled to JSON and consumed by external systems.\\n107\\t//\\n108\\t// Relationship to ValidationError:\\n109\\t// - ValidationError is the internal, comprehensive error structure used by the validate package\\n110\\t// - ValidationErrorData is the external, simplified format returned to API clients\\n111\\t// - Use ToValidationErrorData() to convert ValidationError to ValidationErrorData\\n112\\t//\\n113\\t// # Required Fields\\n114\\t//\\n115\\t// The MessageType, ErrorType, and Message fields are required for all ValidationErrorData.\\n116\\t//\\n117\\t// # Optional Fields\\n118\\t//\\n119\\t// All other fields are optional and should be populated based on the specific validation scenario.\\n120\\t//\\n121\\t// Example usage:\\n122\\t//\\n123\\t//\\tdata := ValidationErrorData{\\n124\\t//\\t    MessageType:  \\\"validation_failed\\\",\\n125\\t//\\t    ErrorType:    \\\"status_code\\\",\\n126\\t//\\t    Message:      \\\"Expected status code 200 but got 404\\\",\\n127\\t//\\t    Context:      \\\"GET /api/users/123\\\",\\n128\\t//\\t    Expected:     200,\\n129\\t//\\t    Actual:       404,\\n130\\t//\\t    Suggestions:  []string{\\\"Check the endpoint URL\\\", \\\"Verify resource exists\\\"},\\n131\\t//\\t}\\n132\\t//\\tjsonBytes, _ := json.Marshal(data)\\n133\\ttype ValidationErrorData struct {\\n134\\t\\t// MessageType is a fixed identifier for this type of error message.\\n135\\t\\t// Always \\\"validation_failed\\\" for ValidationErrorData instances.\\n136\\t\\t// This field allows clients to quickly identify the error category.\\n137\\t\\tMessageType string `json:\\\"message_type\\\"`\\n138\\t\\n139\\t\\t// ErrorType is the specific validation category that failed.\\n140\\t\\t// Common values include: \\\"status_code\\\", \\\"error_message\\\", \\\"content_type\\\",\\n141\\t\\t// \\\"status_code_range\\\", \\\"cors_headers\\\", \\\"response_structure\\\".\\n142\\t\\t// Use the ErrorType* constants for type-safe error type specification.\\n143\\t\\tErrorType string `json:\\\"error_type\\\"`\\n144\\t\\n145\\t\\t// Message is a human-readable description of what went wrong.\\n146\\t\\t// This should be a clear, actionable message that explains the validation failure.\\n147\\t\\tMessage string `json:\\\"message\\\"`\\n148\\t\\n149\\t\\t// Context provides additional information about where or when the validation occurred.\\n150\\t\\t// Examples: \\\"GET /api/users/123\\\", \\\"OAuth token validation\\\", \\\"POST /api/orders\\\"\\n151\\t\\tContext string `json:\\\"context,omitempty\\\"`\\n152\\t\\n153\\t\\t// Expected contains the value that was expected during validation.\\n154\\t\\t// Can be of any type: int, string, []int, etc.\\n155\\t\\tExpected interface{} `json:\\\"expected,omitempty\\\"`\\n156\\t\\n157\\t\\t// Actual contains the value that was actually received during validation.\\n158\\t\\t// Can be of any type: int, string, []int, etc.\\n159\\t\\tActual interface{} `json:\\\"actual,omitempty\\\"`\\n160\\t\\n161\\t\\t// FieldName specifies the field where the error was found (e.g., \\\"error\\\", \\\"message\\\", \\\"detail\\\").\\n162\\t\\t// This is particularly useful for error message validation in response bodies.\\n163\\t\\tFieldName string `json:\\\"field_name,omitempty\\\"`\\n164\\t\\n165\\t\\t// Location specifies where in the input the error occurred.\\n166\\t\\t// Examples: \\\"line 5\\\", \\\"field 'user.email'\\\", \\\"position 123\\\", \\\"header 'Authorization'\\\"\\n167\\t\\tLocation string `json:\\\"location,omitempty\\\"`\\n168\\t\\n169\\t\\t// RelatedFields lists fields related to this error for additional context.\\n170\\t\\t// Examples: [\\\"email\\\", \\\"email_confirmation\\\"], [\\\"access_token\\\", \\\"refresh_token\\\"]\\n171\\t\\tRelatedFields []string `json:\\\"related_fields,omitempty\\\"`\\n172\\t\\n173\\t\\t// PatternDetails contains information about pattern matching failures.\\n174\\t\\t// Example: \\\"regex pattern 'invalid.*token' did not match\\\"\\n175\\t\\tPatternDetails string `json:\\\"pattern_details,omitempty\\\"`\\n176\\t\\n177\\t\\t// RangeInfo specifies range boundaries for range validation failures.\\n178\\t\\t// Example: \\\"400-499 (Client Error)\\\", \\\"200-299 (Success)\\\"\\n179\\t\\tRangeInfo string `json:\\\"range_info,omitempty\\\"`\\n180\\t\\n181\\t\\t// ValidationDetails contains additional validation-specific information.\\n182\\t\\t// This can include multiple details as a list of strings for comprehensive error reporting.\\n183\\t\\tValidationDetails []string `json:\\\"validation_details,omitempty\\\"`\\n184\\t\\n185\\t\\t// ResponseSnippet is a truncated excerpt from the response for debugging.\\n186\\t\\t// This should be limited to a reasonable length (e.g., 200 characters) to keep\\n187\\t\\t// error messages readable while providing useful context.\\n188\\t\\tResponseSnippet string `json:\\\"response_snippet,omitempty\\\"`\\n189\\t\\n190\\t\\t// Suggestions provides actionable recommendations for resolving the validation error.\\n191\\t\\t// When provided, suggestions should be specific and actionable.\\n192\\t\\tSuggestions []string `json:\\\"suggestions,omitempty\\\"`\\n193\\t}\\n194\\t\\n195\\t// Error implements the error interface for ValidationErrorData.\\n196\\t// It returns the Message field for compatibility with error handling patterns.\\n197\\t//\\n198\\t// Example usage:\\n199\\t//\\n200\\t//\\tdata := ValidationErrorData{Message: \\\"Validation failed\\\"}\\n201\\t//\\tlog.Printf(\\\"Error: %v\\\", data)\\n202\\tfunc (ve ValidationErrorData) Error() string {\\n203\\t\\treturn ve.Message\\n204\\t}\\n205\\t\\n206\\t// ToValidationErrorData converts a ValidationError to ValidationErrorData format.\\n207\\t// This function creates a simplified, serialization-friendly version of the validation error\\n208\\t// suitable for external communication and API responses.\\n209\\t//\\n210\\t// The conversion maps ValidationError fields to ValidationErrorData fields,\\n211\\t// with the following special handling:\\n212\\t// - ErrorType is directly copied\\n213\\t// - Message is generated if empty\\n214\\t// - MessageType is always set to \\\"validation_failed\\\"\\n215\\t// - All other fields are preserved if present\\n216\\t//\\n217\\t// Parameters:\\n218\\t//   - ve: The ValidationError to convert\\n219\\t//\\n220\\t// Returns a ValidationErrorData instance with populated fields.\\n221\\t//\\n222\\t// Example usage:\\n223\\t//\\n224\\t//\\tve := ValidationError{\\n225\\t//\\t    ErrorType: \\\"status_code\\\",\\n226\\t//\\t    Expected:  200,\\n227\\t//\\t    Actual:    404,\\n228\\t//\\t    Context:   \\\"GET /api/users\\\",\\n229\\t//\\t}\\n230\\t//\\tdata := ToValidationErrorData(ve)\\n231\\t//\\t// data.MessageType = \\\"validation_failed\\\"\\n232\\t//\\t// data.ErrorType = \\\"status_code\\\"\\n233\\t//\\t// data.Context = \\\"GET /api/users\\\"\\n234\\tfunc ToValidationErrorData(ve ValidationError) ValidationErrorData {\\n235\\t\\t// Generate message if not provided\\n236\\t\\tmessage := ve.Message\\n237\\t\\tif message == \\\"\\\" {\\n238\\t\\t\\t// Generate detailed message with expected/actual values\\n239\\t\\t\\tmessage = generateValidationMessage(ve.ErrorType, ve.Expected, ve.Actual)\\n240\\t\\t}\\n241\\t\\n242\\t\\tdata := ValidationErrorData{\\n243\\t\\t\\tMessageType:  \\\"validation_failed\\\",\\n244\\t\\t\\tErrorType:    ve.ErrorType,\\n245\\t\\t\\tMessage:      message,\\n246\\t\\t\\tContext:      ve.Context,\\n247\\t\\t\\tExpected:     ve.Expected,\\n248\\t\\t\\tActual:       ve.Actual,\\n249\\t\\t\\tFieldName:    ve.FieldName,\\n250\\t\\t\\tLocation:     ve.Location,\\n251\\t\\t\\tRelatedFields:     ve.RelatedFields,\\n252\\t\\t\\tPatternDetails:    ve.PatternDetails,\\n253\\t\\t\\tRangeInfo:         ve.RangeInfo,\\n254\\t\\t\\tValidationDetails: ve.ValidationDetails,\\n255\\t\\t\\tResponseSnippet:   ve.ResponseSnippet,\\n256\\t\\t\\tSuggestions:       ve.Suggestions,\\n257\\t\\t}\\n258\\t\\n259\\t\\treturn data\\n260\\t}\\n261\\t\\n262\\t// generateValidationMessage creates a detailed validation message with expected/actual values.\\n263\\tfunc generateValidationMessage(errorType string, expected, actual interface{}) string {\\n264\\t\\tvar parts []string\\n265\\t\\n266\\t\\t// Add expected value details\\n267\\t\\tif expected != nil {\\n268\\t\\t\\tswitch exp := expected.(type) {\\n269\\t\\t\\tcase int:\\n270\\t\\t\\t\\tparts = append(parts, fmt.Sprintf(\\\"Expected %d (%s)\\\", exp, getStatusCodeDescription(exp)))\\n271\\t\\t\\tcase []int:\\n272\\t\\t\\t\\texpectedStr := \\\"one of [\\\"\\n273\\t\\t\\t\\tfor i, code := range exp {\\n274\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tif i > 0 {\\n275\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\texpectedStr += \\\", \\\"\\n276\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n277\\t\\t\\t\\t\\texpectedStr += data structure for validation error representation.\\n311\\t// It provides a standardized format for validation errors across different\\n312\\t// validation types (status codes, error messages, content types, etc.).\\n313\\t//\\n314\\t// This structure is designed to be:\\n315\\t// - Serializable: Includes JSON tags for marshaling/unmarshaling\\n316\\t// - Machine-readable: Clear field names for automated processing\\n317\\t// - Human-readable: Descriptive messages for debugging\\n318\\t// - Extensible: Optional fields for additional context\\n319\\t//\\n320\\t// # Required Fields\\n321\\t//\\n322\\t// The Message and ErrorType fields are required for all validation errors.\\n323\\t//\\n324\\t// # Optional Fields\\n325\\t//\\n326\\t// All other fields are optional and \\\"GET /api/users/123\\\",\\n337\\t//\\t}\\n338\\t//\\tjsonBytes, _ := json.Marshal(err)\\n339\\ttype ValidationError struct {\\n340\\t\\t// ErrorType is the specific validation category that failed.\\n341\\t\\t// This field identifies what kind of validation was performed and failed.\\n342\\t\\t// Common values include: \\\"status_code\\\", \\\"error_message\\\", message that explains the validation failure.\\n350\\t\\t// The message should be concise but informative enough for debugging.\\n351\\t\\t//\\n352\\t\\t// This field is required for all validation errors.\\n353\\t\\tMessage string `json:\\\"message\\\"`\\n354\\t\\n355\\t\\t// Context provides additional information about where or when the validation occurred.\\n356\\t\\t// This can include {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_199f5527580d465a982fb32f","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# ARMOR\n2\t\n3\t**Authenticated Range-readable Managed Object Repository**\n4\t\n5\tARMOR is an S3-compatible proxy server that transparently encrypts data before storing it in [Backblaze B2](https://www.backblaze.com/cloud-storage) and serves downloads through Cloudflare for zero-egress cost. Any S3-compatible client — boto3, AWS CLI, DuckDB, rclone — works without modification.\n6\t\n7\t- **Zero-knowledge encryption** — data is encrypted before it leaves ARMOR; B2 only ever stores ciphertext\n8\t- **Zero egress fees** — downloads route through Cloudflare via the Bandwidth Alliance\n9\t- **Seekable encryption** — AES-256-CTR with 64KB blocks enables byte-range reads without decrypting the whole file\n10\t- **DuckDB-compatible** — query encrypted Parquet files with column pruning and predicate pushdown intact\n11\t- **Multi-key routing** — different master keys for different path prefixes; automatic key selection per object\n12\t\n13\t## Quick Start\n14\t\n15\t### Docker\n16\t\n17\t```bash\n18\tdocker run -d \\\n19\t  -p 9000:9000 \\\n20\t  -p 9001:9001 \\\n21\t  -e ARMOR_B2_REGION=us-east-005 \\\n22\t  -e ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your-key-id \\\n23\t  -e ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your-key-secret \\\n24\t  -e ARMOR_BUCKET=your-bucket \\\n25\t  -e ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN=your-cf-domain.example.com \\\n26\t  -e ARMOR_MEK=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \\\n27\t  -e ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY=my-access-key \\\n28\t  -e ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY=my-secret-key \\\n29\t  ronaldraygun/armor:0.1.43\n30\t```\n31\t\n32\t> **Note:** The ARMOR CI pipeline auto-bumps the VERSION file on every build and publishes the container image as `ronaldraygun/armor:<version>` to Docker Hub. Always pin to a specific version tag in production deployments. Use the latest published tag from [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/r/ronaldraygun/armor/tags).\n33\t\n34\t### Client Configuration\n35\t\n36\tPoint any S3-compatible tool at ARMOR's listen address:\n37\t\n38\t```bash\n39\t# AWS CLI\n40\taws --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3 cp file.txt s3://bucket/key\n41\t\n42\t# boto3 (Python)\n43\timport boto3\n44\ts3 = boto3.client('s3',\n45\t    endpoint_url='http://localhost:9000',\n46\t    aws_access_key_id='my-access-key',\n47\t    aws_secret_access_key='my-secret-key')\n48\ts3.upload_file('local.txt', 'bucket', 'key')\n49\t\n50\t# DuckDB\n51\tINSTALL httpfs;\n52\tLOAD httpfs;\n53\tSET s3_endpoint='localhost:9000';\n54\tSET s3_access_key_id='my-access-key';\n55\tSET s3_secret_access_key='my-secret-key';\n56\tSELECT * FROM read_parquet('s3://bucket/data.parquet');\n57\t```\n58\t\n59\t## Cost Model\n60\t\n61\t| Component | Cost |\n62\t|-----------|------|\n63\t| Storage | ~$6–7/TB/month |\n64\t| Egress (via Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance) | $0 |\n65\t| B2 API calls | $0 |\n66\t| Cloudflare (free plan) | $0 |\n67\t| **Total** | **~$6–7/TB/month** |\n68\t\n69\t## Architecture\n70\t\n71\t### Upload Path (direct to B2 — ingress is free)\n72\t\n73\t```\n74\t┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n75\t│  Client   │────▶│    ARMOR     │────▶│    B2    │\n76\t│           │     │  encrypt +   │     │          │\n77\t│           │     │  upload      │     │          │\n78\t└──────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┘\n79\t```\n80\t\n81\t### Download Path (through Cloudflare — egress is free)\n82\t\n83\t```\n84\t┌──────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n85\t│  Client   │◀───│ Cloudflare │◀───│  Cloudflare │◀───│    B2    │\n86\t│  ARMOR    │    │   Edge     │    │  PNI Link   │    │          │\n87\t│  decrypt  │    │  (cache)   │    │  (free)     │    │          │\n88\t└──────────┘     └────────────┘     └────────────┘     └──────────┘\n89\t```\n90\t\n91\t### DuckDB Query Path (seekable decryption)\n92\t\n93\tDuckDB issues byte-range GET requests for specific row groups and columns. ARMOR decrypts only the requested 64KB blocks, so column pruning and predicate pushdown remain effective:\n94\t\n95\t```\n96\tDuckDB                          ARMOR                       Cloudflare → B2\n97\t  │                                │                              │\n98\t  ├─ read footer (last 8 bytes) ──▶├─ Range GET (encrypted) ────▶│\n99\t  │◀── decrypted footer ──────────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n100\t  │                                │                              │"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"call_2a11f0cdbd5c4710bfb3e37a","session_id":"16e98a08-e278-4370-a6e7-7a92aad28e91","uuid":"f698a3d3-ad2f-46be-be6c-1498103fc97f","timestamp":"2026-07-15T17:03:57.282Z","subagent_type":"Explore","task_description":"Explore ARMOR codebase structure"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_059c9fa74ccd4474942f0798","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# ARMOR Error Testing Framework Guide\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThe ARMOR Error Testing Framework is a comprehensive, pattern-based testing infrastructure for validating S3-compatible error responses. This guide is designed for new developers to understand how to use, extend, and maintain the error testing system.\n6\t\n7\t## Table of Contents\n8\t\n9\t1. [Architecture Overview](#architecture-overview)\n10\t2. [Core Concepts](#core-concepts)\n11\t3. [Quick Start Guide](#quick-start-guide)\n12\t4. [Using Predefined Patterns](#using-predefined-patterns)\n13\t5. [Creating Custom Patterns](#creating-custom-patterns)\n14\t6. [Test Infrastructure](#test-infrastructure)\n15\t7. [Validation Helpers](#validation-helpers)\n16\t8. [Best Practices](#best-practices)\n17\t9. [Common Patterns](#common-patterns)\n18\t10. [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)\n19\t\n20\t## Architecture Overview\n21\t\n22\tThe error testing framework is organized into several interconnected files:\n23\t\n24\t```\n25\tinternal/server/\n26\t├── error_test_patterns.go              # Core pattern definitions\n27\t├── error_test_infrastructure_test.go   # Test helpers and fixtures\n28\t├── error_test_patterns_base_test.go    # Test table structures\n29\t├── error_test_patterns_test.go         # Framework tests\n30\t├── http_error_fixtures.go              # HTTP response fixtures\n31\t└── error_pattern_usage_example.go      # Usage examples\n32\t```\n33\t\n34\t### File Responsibilities\n35\t\n36\t| File | Purpose |\n37\t|------|---------|\n38\t| `error_test_patterns.go` | Defines all error pattern structures, constants, and predefined patterns |\n39\t| `error_test_infrastructure_test.go` | Provides test server fixtures, validation helpers, and utility functions |\n40\t| `error_test_patterns_base_test.go` | Contains test table structures and predefined test tables |\n41\t| `error_test_patterns_test.go` | Tests for the framework itself |\n42\t| `http_error_fixtures.go` | HTTP error response fixtures for integration testing |\n43\t| `error_pattern_usage_example.go` | Executable examples demonstrating pattern usage |\n44\t\n45\t## Core Concepts\n46\t\n47\t### 1. Error Patterns\n48\t\n49\tError patterns are reusable configurations that define expected error responses. They encapsulate:\n50\t\n51\t- **HTTP Status Code**: The expected HTTP status (e.g., 404, 403)\n52\t- **S3 Error Code**: The S3-specific error code (e.g., \"NoSuchKey\")\n53\t- **Message Validation**: Expected message content and keywords\n54\t- **Performance Expectations**: Maximum acceptable response time\n55\t- **Category**: The error type (Auth, NotFound, InvalidRequest, etc.)\n56\t\n57\t### 2. Test Tables\n58\t\n59\tTest tables are collections of test cases that use error patterns. They enable:\n60\t\n61\t- **Table-Driven Testing**: Run multiple scenarios with consistent validation\n62\t- **Composability**: Combine base patterns with custom scenarios\n63\t- **Organization**: Group related tests by category or feature\n64\t\n65\t### 3. Validation Chain\n66\t\n67\tThe framework provides a fluent validation API for checking responses:\n68\t\n69\t```go\n70\tVerifyErrorResponse(t, response).\n71\t    HTTPStatusCode(404).\n72\t    HasCode(\"NoSuchKey\").\n73\t    MessageContainsAny(\"not\", \"found\").\n74\t    Assert()\n75\t```\n76\t\n77\t## Quick Start Guide\n78\t\n79\t### Step 1: Use a Predefined Pattern\n80\t\n81\tThe simplest way to start is using predefined patterns:\n82\t\n83\t```go\n84\timport \"github.com/jedarden/armor/internal/server\"\n85\t\n86\tfunc TestMyFeatureErrors(t *testing.T) {\n87\t    fixture := server.NewTestServer(t)\n88\t    \n89\t    // Use the ResourceNotFound pattern\n90\t    pattern := server.CommonErrorPatterns.ResourceNotFound\n91\t    \n92\t    // Make a request\n93\t    req := server.CreateTestRequest(t, \"GET\", \"/bucket/nonexistent\", nil, nil)\n94\t    duration, w := server.MeasureRequestTime(fixture.Handler, req)\n95\t    \n96\t    // Validate using pattern expectations\n97\t    server.VerifyErrorResponseWithTiming(t, w, duration).\n98\t        HTTPStatusCode(pattern.ExpectedStatus).\n99\t        HasCode(pattern.ExpectedCode).\n100\t        MessageContainsAny(pattern.ExpectedKeywords...).\n101\t        Assert()\n102\t}\n103\t```\n104\t\n105\t### Step 2: Run a Predefined Test Table\n106\t\n107\tFor comprehensive testing, use predefined test tables:\n108\t\n109\t```go\n110\tfunc TestAuthenticationErrors(t *testing.T) {\n111\t    fixture := server.NewTestServer(t)\n112\t    tests := server.StandardAuthenticationErrorTests()\n113\t    \n114\t    for _, tt := range tests {\n115\t        t.Run(tt.Name, func(t *testing.T) {\n116\t            server.RunAuthenticationTestCase(t, fixture, tt)\n117\t        })\n118\t    }\n119\t}\n120\t```\n121\t\n122\t### Step 3: Extend with Custom Tests\n123\t\n124\tAdd your custom tests to the base patterns:\n125\t\n126\t```go\n127\tfunc TestCustomScenarios(t *testing.T) {\n128\t    fixture := server.NewTestServer(t)\n129\t    \n130\t    // Start with base patterns\n131\t    baseTests := server.StandardAuthenticationErrorTests()\n132\t    \n133\t    // Add custom tests\n134\t    customTests := []server.AuthenticationErrorTestCase{\n135\t        {\n136\t            CommonErrorTestCase: server.CommonErrorTestCase{\n137\t                Name:        \"My custom auth scenario\",\n138\t                Description: \"Tests my specific authentication logic\",\n139\t                SetupRequest: func(t *testing.T) *http.Request {\n140\t                    return createMyCustomRequest(t)\n141\t                },\n142\t                ExpectedStatus: 403,\n143\t                ExpectedCode:   server.ErrorCodeAccessDenied,\n144\t            },\n145\t            AuthErrorType: \"CustomAuthFailure\",\n146\t        },\n147\t    }\n148\t    \n149\t    // Combine and run\n150\t    allTests := append(baseTests, Main Source Code Directories\n\n**Go Application Structure:**\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/server/` - Main S3-compatible HTTP server and error handling\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/server/handlers/` - S3 operation handlers (PutObject, GetObject, etc.)\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/backend/` - B2 backend integration and caching\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/config/` - Configuration management\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/validate/` - Comprehensive validation error types and helpers\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/crypto/` - Encryption/decryption operations\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/cmd/armor/` - Main server entry point\n\n### 2. Existing Test Infrastructure\n\n**Go Tests:**\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/server/error_test_patterns.go` - Core error pattern definitions\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/server/error_testing_base.go` - Base test infrastructure  \n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/server/error_testing_helpers.go` - ARMOR-specific test helpers\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/server/error_test_infrastructure_test.go` - Framework tests\n\n**Python Tests:**\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/tests/test_armor_error_responses.py` - S3 error response XML validation\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/tests/test_helpers.py` - HTTP validation helper functions\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/tests/test_error_response_validation.py` - Error response validation\n- `/home/coding/ARMOR/tests/test_http_validation_helpers.py` - HTTP validation utilities\n\n### 3. Error Types and Response Structures\n\n**Core Error Types (`/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/validate/error_types.go`):**\n- `ValidationError` - Comprehensive validation error structure with JSON serialization\n- `ValidationErrorData` - Simplified external API error format\n- Error categorization with constants (status_code, error_message, content_type, etc.)\n\n**S3 Error Response Format:**\n- XML-based error responses with `Code` and `Message` fields\n- Standard S3 error codes (NoSuchKey, AccessDenied, InternalError, etc.)\n- HTTP status code mapping to S3 error categories\n\n### 4. Test Helper Functions\n\n**Go Test Helpers:**\n- `TestARMORBlobNotFound()` - Test 404 scenarios\n- `TestARMORAuthFailure()` - Test authentication failures  \n- `ValidateS3ErrorResponse()` - Validate S3 error structure\n- `MakeGETRequest()` - HTTP request helper\n- `VerifyErrorResponse()` - Fluent validation API\n\n**Python Test Helpers:**\n- `validate_s3_error_response()` - S3 XML validation\n- `validate_xml_well_formedness()` - XML structure validation\n- `extract_s3_error_code()` - Extract error codes from responses\n- `StatusValidationError` - Custom assertion errors\n\n### 5. HTTP Handlers and API Endpoints\n\n**Main Handler File:** `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/server/handlers/handlers.go`\n- `HandleRoot()` - Main S3 operation router\n- `PutObject()` - Object upload with encryption\n- `GetObject()` - Object download with decryption\n- `DeleteObject()` - Object deletion\n- `ListObjectsV2()` - Bucket listing\n- `CopyObject()` - Server-side copy operations\n- Multipart upload operations (CreateMultipartUpload, UploadPart, CompleteMultipartUpload)\n\n**Error Response Writer:**\n```go\nfunc (h *Handlers) writeError(w http.ResponseWriter, code, message string, statusCode int)\n```\nWrites S3-compatible XML error responses with proper format and headers.\n\n### 6. Project Entry Points\n\n**Server Entry:** `/home/coding/ARMOR/cmd/armor/main.go`\n- Creates server instance via `server.New(cfg)`\n- Sets up HTTP and admin servers\n- Handles graceful shutdown\n\n**Configuration:** `/home/coding/ARMOR/internal/config/config.go`\n- Environment variable configuration\n- Authentication credentials management\n- Multi-key routing support\n\n### 7. Key Test Patterns\n\nThe project uses a sophisticated error testing framework with:\n- **Pattern-based testing** - Predefined error response patterns\n- **Table-driven tests** - Comprehensive test tables for different scenarios\n- **Validation chains** - Fluent API for response validation\n- **Performance expectations** - Response time validation\n\n### 8. Documentation\n\n**Comprehensive docs in `/home/coding/ARMOR/docs/`:**\n- `error-testing-framework-guide.md` - Complete testing framework guide\n- `error-responses.md` - Error response specifications\n- `s3-error-response-compliance-analysis.md` - S3 compliance analysis\n- `test-table-extension-guide.md` - Extending test tables\n\nThis codebase has excellent separation of concerns, comprehensive test infrastructure, and well-documented error handling patterns that make it ideal for adding additional error response tests. =============================================================================\n# HELPER FUNCTIONS FOR ASSERTING ERROR STRUCTURE AND STATUS CODES\n# =============================================================================\n\ndef create_s3_xml_error_response(error_code: str, message: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    Create a properly formatted S3 XML error response.\n\n    Args:\n        error_code: The S3 error code (e.g., 'NoSuchKey', 'AccessDenied')\n        message: The error message\n\n    Returns:\n        str: Valid S3 XML error response\n    \"\"\"\n    return f'''<?xml version=\"1.0\" encoding=\"UTF-8\"?>\n<Error>\n  <Code>{error_code}</Code>\n  <Message>{message}</Message>\n</Error>'''\n\n\ndef create_mock_response(\n    status_code: int,\n    error_code: str,\n    message: str,\n    content_type: str = 'application/xml'\n) -> MockHTTPResponse:\n    \"\"\"\n    Create a mock HTTP response with proper S3 XML error body.\n\n    Args:\n        status_code: HTTP status code\n        error_code: S3 error code\n        message: Error message\n        content_type: Content-Type header value\n\n    Returns:\n MockHTTPResponse,\n    expected_status_code: int,\n    expected_error_code: str,\n    message_contains: Optional[str] = None\n) -> None:\n    \"\"\"\n    Assert HTTP status code and XML error response structure.\n\n    This is the main helper function for validating both the HTTP status code\n    and the XML error response structure. It performs comprehensive validation\n    including:\n\n    1. HTTP status code matches expected value\n    2. Response body is valid XML\n    3. XML contains required <Code> and <Message> fields\n    4. Error code matches expected value\n    5. error code\n        message_contains: Optional string that should be in the error message\n\n    Raises:\n        AssertionError: If any validation fails\n    \"\"\"\n    # Validate HTTP status code\n    assert response.status_code == expected_status_code, (\n        f\"Expected status code {expected_status_code}, got {response.status_code}\"\n    )\n\n    # Validate Content-Type header\n    content_type = response.headers.get('Content-Type', '')\n    assert 'application/xml' in content_type or 'text/xml' in content_type, (\n        f\"Expected XML Content-Type, got '{content_type}'\"\n    )\n\n    # Validate XML error response structure\n    is_valid_xml = validate_s3_error_response(\n        response.body,\n        throw_on_error=False\n    )\n    assert is_valid_xml, (\n        f\"Invalid XML error response structure for status {expected_status_code}\"\n    )\n\n    # Validate error code matches\n    actual_code = extract_s3_error_code(response.body)\n    assert actual_code      message = extract_s3_error_message(response.body)\n        assert message_contains.lower() in message.lower(), (\n            f\"Expected message to contain '{message_contains}', got with expected values.\n\n    Args:\n        response: HTTP response to validate\n        required_headers: Dictionary of required header names and values\n\n    Raises:\n        AssertionError: If any required header is missing or has wrong value\n    \"\"\"\n    for header_name, expected_value in required_headers.items():\n        actual_value = response.headers.get(header_name)\n        assert
